column18: You will be able to proceed after 60 seconds. Please take this time to familiarize yourself with the application. You may take longer than 60 seconds if you wish.  You may navigate through different map views using the navigation on the LEFT side. It can be opened by moving the mouse to the left side of the screen or by clicking the menu button (≡) in the top left corner. At the top of the navigation you can see a "MyView" feature which allows you to display information from the other maps in one. You can select which maps you wish to see through the search bar on the right.    Map views can be interacted with through the map itself or the sidebar on the RIGHT side of the screen.    Please keep the link open for the entire duration of the study.

column20: Please complete the following tasks using the application.   The tasks are kept intentionally vague. The exact location and details of events are irrelevant - focus on the task at hand. You will have 3min to complete each task.     You may be asked to upload a screenshot at some point. Please make sure you are capable of taking and uploading screenshots.If no input (e.g. ticking a radio button) has been made in those three minutes, the task will count as not completed.
column21: There is currently an evacuation going on somewhere in Hamburg. The chief of police needs to know where!  When asked for details, she provides you with the name of the evacuation incident: "Castle on Fire!".     On the map below, pinpoint where this incident is taking place. Your selection is valid as long as the coordinates are roughly within those of the evacuation zone.

column17: Yes, I have taken part in a smaller regional-level emergency response to severe weather and flooding where I was involved in locating and providing accommodation to displaced individuals and families. I have also taken part in a significant national-level emergency response to a severe cyclone, where I was in a bunker under government buildings organising the transport and accommodation of emergency responders into the affected locations. I have also taken part in multiple other severe weather related responses, providing general support.
column23: Very easy to locate evacuation zone, I looked at RESCUE-MATE, grabbed the name of the nearest town and then searched for a location in the middle of the evacuation zone.
column27: Easy to locate where to add a new incident. Clicking and dragging the incident area was a bit difficult, as it was initially placed not where I intended it, so had to drag each point so it covered the area I wanted. I completed the incident report, and my incident area did not show up immediately, I had to go into the situation map and then back into Incidents and Evacuations to try "refresh" the map, which is when it appeared.
column30: I struggled with this one a little, I think partially because I am unfamiliar with the location and the map, and this is the first time I've ever come across the term "polder" too, so had to take a second to realise what was being referenced. I then realised I needed to open the Polder map, where I was quickly able to locate and view #7. Unfortunately, the water rescue points data was in another map, so went back to the situation map and turned on the layer for water rescue points. Then, I forgot where the border of Polder 7, so went back to the Polder map, then when I came back into the situation map it zoomed me back out, so had to zoom in and try locate where Polder 7 was. I did come to the conclusion that there was 4, but I was far too slow. Ideally, would be great if all Polder-related information like water rescue points could be included as a layer in the Polder map.
column33: Very easy to scroll down the right-hand side to get to Polder 73. Modifying the data was simple, super easy to click the relevant data in the drop-downs and save changes.
column36: Super quick and easy. A feature that counts all items in the selected layer for the visible map, like in ArcGIS, would be a time saver if there was lots of stations.
column40: I went into the ship map and ended up with all the ships at once for a start. Used the right-hand side to hide all ships, searched for MS Helenaria and toggled it to view, and then located, clicked the ship on the map and located the MMSI number, then copy paste. Easy!
column46: You specified in the question regarding fire stations that any I could see around Hamburg could be counted and to not worry about trying to figure out the city limits. If you hadn't, having a layer or information on where the city ends would have been very useful. Having the ability to search the map by town or address could be really helpful for someone like me who is not familiar with Hamburg at all.
column64: No suggestions come to mind, I think it's great. A really simple way to be able to view, modify and add data to a map.
column65: I know for us, our geospatial system holds data on many things that prove to be very helpful, such as number of residents (and general demographic data) within a suburb or city, tsunami evacuation zones, locations of resources, location information on our critical lifelines (gas, power, transportation and outages) - so many options. We also have a collection of view-only dashboards, great for those who aren't as tech-savvy, and also handy for briefings.
